# Did the 1918 influenza pandemic accelerate the adoption of public health infrastructure?

The pandemic accelerated some public health investments and had little measurable effect on others. The clearest acceleration is in permanent municipal health departments and in the professionalization of public health as a discipline; claims about sanitation infrastructure are weaker, because most of that spending was already underway.

## What accelerated

Municipal health departments expanded rapidly after 1918. Tomes (1998) documents that the number of US cities with permanently staffed health departments roughly doubled between 1917 and 1923, and that budget lines which had been temporary emergency appropriations were converted to recurring ones in many of them.

Professional training followed the same pattern. The Johns Hopkins School of Hygiene and Public Health opened in 1918 and several comparable schools followed within a decade, most funded by the Rockefeller Foundation, which had committed to the model before the pandemic but expanded the commitment after it.

## What did not

Water and sewer investment shows no clear break at 1918. Cutler and Miller (2005) date the large US municipal water treatment expansion to roughly 1900-1920 and attribute the associated mortality decline primarily to filtration and chlorination adopted before the pandemic. The pandemic falls inside that window rather than initiating it.

Hospital construction likewise follows a trend that predates 1918, driven by the shift of surgery and childbirth into institutional settings.

## What the evidence cannot settle

Separating the pandemic's effect from the general Progressive Era expansion of municipal government is genuinely difficult, because the two coincide and no natural experiment cleanly separates them. Studies exploiting variation in local pandemic severity find smaller effects than national time-series comparisons, which is the pattern expected when a common trend is being partly attributed to the shock.

## Sources

- Tomes, N. (1998). *The Gospel of Germs: Men, Women, and the Microbe in American Life*. Harvard University Press.
- Cutler, D., & Miller, G. (2005). The Role of Public Health Improvements in Health Advances: The Twentieth-Century United States. *Demography*, 42(1), 1-22.
- Fee, E. (1987). *Disease and Discovery: A History of the Johns Hopkins School of Hygiene and Public Health, 1916-1939*. Johns Hopkins University Press.
